questionText: Exam marks, $X$, are modelled by $X \sim N(60, 12^2)$. Using the $68$–$95$–$99.7$ rule, estimate the proportion of candidates scoring between $48$ and $72$ marks.

steps.explanation: The marks are normal with mean $\mu = 60$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 12$.

steps.description: Compare the limits with the mean and sd
steps.explanation: The two limits are exactly one standard deviation either side of the mean, so the interval is $\mu \pm \sigma$.

steps.description: Recall the relevant part of the rule
steps.explanation: The $68$–$95$–$99.7$ rule says about $68\%$ of a normal distribution lies within one standard deviation of the mean.

steps.description: Apply the rule to this interval
steps.explanation: Because $48$ and $72$ are precisely $\mu \pm \sigma$, the required probability is the $68\%$ figure from the rule.
So roughly $68$ out of every $100$ candidates score between $48$ and $72$ marks.
This is the estimated proportion, obtained directly from the empirical rule without any detailed standardising.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: About $0.68$ (approximately $68\%$) of candidates score between $48$ and $72$ marks.
